Version
  • Theory
  • Heat Transfer Correlations
  • Heat Transfer Correlations

Heat Transfer Correlations

All correlations are taken from the Greenhouses Modelica library (Altes-Buch 2019), which follows the greenhouse climate model of Vanthoor et al. (2011) and de Zwart (1996).

Exterior convection (combined forced + natural)

The convective heat transfer coefficient on the outer surface of the cover combines a wind-driven (forced) part and a buoyancy-driven (natural) part, following the DOE-2 quadratic combination:

\[h_{ext} = \frac{\sqrt{h_{forced}^2 + h_{natural}^2}}{\cos\varphi}\]

Forced part (Bot 1983)

\[h_{forced}(u) = \begin{cases} 2.8 + 1.2\,u & \text{if } u < 4 \text{ m/s} \\ 2.5\,u^{0.8} & \text{if } u \geq 4 \text{ m/s} \end{cases}\]

A smooth logistic transition is applied at $u = 4$ m/s for differentiability:

\[f = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-11(u - 4)}}, \qquad h_{forced} = (1 - f)\,h_a + f\,h_b\]

Natural part (McAdams horizontal hot surface)

\[h_{natural} = 1.52 \cdot |T_{cov} - T_{out}|^{1/3}\]

The natural-convection term is essential at low wind: without it the cover loses 30–50 % less heat to the outside than EnergyPlus / DOE-2 expect, which leaves the greenhouse a few degrees too hot during the day in sheltered climates (Chambéry summer wind ≈ 2 m/s).

The $1/\cos\varphi$ factor accounts for the increased convective exposure of a tilted surface (roof slope $\varphi$).

Sources: Bot, G.P.A. (1983), PhD thesis, Wageningen; de Zwart, H.F. (1996), PhD thesis, Wageningen; McAdams, W.H. (1954) Heat Transmission; ASHRAE Fundamentals (Surface Heat Balance).

Interior free convection (Nusselt-Rayleigh)

For heat exchange between the indoor air and the cover:

\[h_{int}(\Delta T, \varphi) = 1.7 \cdot |\Delta T|^{0.33} \cdot (\cos\varphi)^{-0.66}\]

Source: Balemans, L. (1989). PhD thesis, Ghent.

Bidirectional floor convection

For the floor, the convection coefficient depends on the direction of heat flow (FreeConvection.mo):

  • Upward (floor warmer than air, $\Delta T > 0$): buoyancy-aided

\[h_{up} = 1.7 \cdot |\Delta T|^{0.33}\]

  • Downward (air warmer than floor, $\Delta T < 0$): buoyancy-opposed

\[h_{down} = 1.3 \cdot |\Delta T|^{0.25}\]

A smooth sigmoid transition ($s = 11$) blends between the two:

\[h_{floor} = f_{up} \cdot h_{up} + (1 - f_{up}) \cdot h_{down}, \quad f_{up} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-11 \cdot \Delta T}}\]

The downward coefficient (exponent 0.25) is weaker than upward (exponent 0.33), which is physically correct: warm air rising from a hot floor creates strong buoyancy, while warm air settling onto a cold floor suppresses convection.

Source: Balemans (1989), implemented in FreeConvection.mo lines 59–61.

Wall inside film (vertical surface)

For the inside face of vertical glazed walls the inside film coefficient is temperature-dependent, with an offset for the buoyancy-driven recirculation induced by the hot floor:

\[h_{in,wall} = 1.7 \cdot |T_{air} - T_{wall}|^{0.33} + 1.0\]

This gives ~5 W/(m²·K) at $\Delta T = 20$ K, matching typical TARP / ASHRAE values for vertical glazing.

Wall outside film (DOE-2 / ASHRAE for vertical surfaces)

\[h_{out,wall} = 5.7 + 3.8\,u_{wind} \quad [\mathrm{W/(m^2 \cdot K)}]\]

This is the Walton (1981) windward correlation. The original Bot 1983 cover formula is not applied to walls because it's calibrated for tilted glass roofs, not vertical glazing.

Why two different correlations

Bot 1983 was tuned to greenhouse roof measurements (tilted, mostly forced); Walton 1981 was tuned to building façades (vertical, mixed). Mixing them is physically correct: the cover and the walls obey different flow regimes.

Pipe convection (PipeFreeConvection.mo)

Heating pipes use position-dependent correlations from Bot (1983) and Balemans (1989):

Free pipe (overhead, above canopy)

\[\alpha = 1.28 \times d^{-0.25} \times |\Delta T|^{0.25} \quad [W/(m^2_{pipe} \cdot K)]\]

Depends on pipe diameter $d$ [m]. Larger pipes have lower specific convection due to thicker boundary layers.

Source: Balemans (1989), PipeFreeConvection.mo line 22.

Hindered pipe (under canopy, rail, under bench)

\[\alpha = 1.99 \times |\Delta T|^{0.32} \quad [W/(m^2_{pipe} \cdot K)]\]

Diameter-independent. The higher exponent (0.32 vs 0.25) gives stronger convection at large $\Delta T$.

Source: Bot (1983), PipeFreeConvection.mo line 24.

View factor (HeatingPipe.mo)

The pipe-to-surroundings radiation view factor:

\[FF_{pipe} = \frac{N_p \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot l}{A_{floor}} \times c\]

Position$c$Source
Free (overhead)0.50HeatingPipe.mo
Hindered (under canopy / bench / rail)0.49HeatingPipe.mo

Pipe radiation with intermediate obstruction

When multiple surfaces exist between pipe and target (canopy, screen, other pipes), each reduces the view:

\[Q_{rad} = A \cdot \varepsilon_a \varepsilon_b \cdot FF_a \cdot FF_b \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{n}(1 - FF_k) \cdot \sigma(T_a^4 - T_b^4)\]

Source: Radiation_T4.mo (de Zwart 1996).

Pipe positions in practice

PositionSymbolConvectionResponse time $\tau$
Overhead (above canopy):highFree (Balemans)~73 min
Growth-level (under canopy):lowHindered (Bot)~76 min
Rail (near floor):lowHindered (Bot)~76 min
Under bench (soilless):under_benchHindered (Bot)~52 min
Floor-embedded:floorConduction-dominated~25 min
Under-bench heating

No specific convection correlation has been published for pipes in the confined space under growing benches. The hindered correlation from Bot (1983) is the closest validated model. The real benefit of under-bench heating is operational: lowering the air setpoint by 3–5 °C while maintaining root zone temperature, achieving 10–25% energy savings (Kim et al. 2024).

Long-wave radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann)

The radiative heat flux between two surfaces is:

\[q_{rad} = \varepsilon_a \, \varepsilon_b \, F_a \, F_b \, \sigma \, (T_a^4 - T_b^4)\]

where:

  • $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant
  • $\varepsilon_a, \varepsilon_b$ are the surface emissivities
  • $F_a, F_b$ are view factors
  • Temperatures are in Kelvin
Surface$\varepsilon$Source
Glass cover0.84Vanthoor (2011)
Concrete floor0.89Vanthoor (2011)
Heating pipes0.88Radiation_T4.mo
Canopy leaves1.00Stanghellini (1987)
Thermal screen1.00de Zwart (1996)
Sky1.00Blackbody approximation

Geometric view factors to sky and ground

The helper view_factor_to_sky(tilt_rad) returns the analytic view factor of an isolated planar surface to an isotropic sky dome:

\[F_{sky} = \frac{1 + \cos\varphi}{2}\]

Surface$\varphi$$F_{sky}$$F_{ground}$
Horizontal up-facing (flat roof)01.000.00
Tilted Venlo cover25°0.950.05
Savona pitched cover30°0.930.07
Vertical wall90°0.500.50

The complementary $1 - F_{sky}$ is the view factor to the ground, used for the wall-to-ground (and tilted-cover-to-ground) infrared exchange. Both terms are computed: a vertical wall sees half of the cold sky and half of the (warmer) ground, and ignoring either side biases the night-time heat balance by 1–3 °C in continental climates.

Cover and wall radiative balances

Cover (with night cover and thermal screen factors omitted for clarity):

\[Q_{rad,cov \to sky} = A_{cov} \cdot \varepsilon_{cov} \cdot F_{cov,sky} \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_{cov}^4 - T_{sky}^4)\]

\[Q_{rad,cov \to ground} = A_{cov} \cdot \varepsilon_{cov} \cdot (1 - F_{cov,sky}) \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_{cov}^4 - T_{out}^4)\]

Wall (when A_walls > 0):

\[Q_{rad,wall \to sky} = A_{walls} \cdot \varepsilon_{cov} \cdot 0.5 \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_{wall}^4 - T_{sky}^4)\]

\[Q_{rad,wall \to ground} = A_{walls} \cdot \varepsilon_{cov} \cdot 0.5 \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_{wall}^4 - T_{out}^4)\]

The cover-to-sky radiation (28 W/m² in winter) is the dominant loss mechanism in an unheated greenhouse and the primary cause of the sub-cooling depression. The wall-to-ground term has a smaller magnitude per m² but matters disproportionately for small greenhouses where $A_{walls} \gtrsim A_{floor}$.
